ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Meen Moilee; 'fish curry dish' (FOODON_03302050) is the closest parent category covering coconut-milk-based fish curries.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Meen Moilee with Parotta as a composite dish. A0CKP (Fish, cooked) is used as the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component. The parotta component is not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Meen Moilee or Kerala fish curry as a clinical food entity.

main_ingredients: fish (white fish, typically seer fish or pomfret)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Meen Moilee with Parotta consists of approximately 200–250 g of coconut milk fish curry and 2 pieces of parotta (approximately 180–200 g total), yielding a combined plate weight of approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods: fish curry (coconut milk base) and parotta components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Meen Moilee with Parotta as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for (1) fish curry in coconut milk (approximately 55% of plate weight) and (2) parotta / layered wheat flatbread (approximately 45% of plate weight). Fish curry per 100 g reference: energy ~95 kcal, protein ~9 g, fat ~5 g, carbohydrate ~3 g. Parotta per 100 g reference: energy ~320 kcal, protein ~7 g, fat ~12 g, carbohydrate ~46 g. Weighted composite at 55:45 ratio yields the per-100g values reported. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as secondary cross-check for fish and coconut milk components.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of parotta / layered wheat flatbread analogous to white wheat bread composite; fish curry component contributes negligible GI load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Meen Moilee with Parotta. The composite GI is estimated as a carbohydrate-weighted average: parotta (analogous to refined wheat flatbread, GI ~70 per University of Sydney GI Database for white wheat bread / chapati analogues) contributes ~90% of the glycemic carbohydrate load; the fish curry coconut milk component contributes ~10% with a low GI (~35 for coconut milk-based dishes). Weighted composite GI = (0.90 × 70) + (0.10 × 35) = 63 + 3.5 ≈ 62. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (62 × 50.4) / 100 ≈ 31.2, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 22.5 × 4.5 = 101.25 g total, but digestible carbohydrate from parotta portion (~200 g × 0.46 = 92 g) adjusted for fibre yields ~50.4 g net glycemic carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1395 mg per full serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving or fewer parotta pieces, as the full restaurant serving provides approximately 1395 mg sodium — nearly 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg — primarily from the curry sauce and salted parotta dough.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load from parotta (GI ~62, GL ~31 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this meal due to the high glycemic load driven by refined wheat parotta; reducing to one piece of parotta and increasing the fish curry proportion can substantially lower the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.5 g per 100 g) and refined carbohydrate from parotta
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that the coconut milk base contributes medium-chain saturated fatty acids and the parotta adds refined carbohydrates; choosing a smaller portion and pairing with a vegetable side can help manage saturated fat and total energy intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Meen Moilee with Parotta is a classic Kerala-style meal pairing a delicate coconut milk fish curry with flaky layered flatbread. Meen Moilee (also spelled Meen Molly) is one of Kerala's most celebrated fish preparations: fresh white fish — typically seer fish (king mackerel), pomfret, or pearl spot — is gently simmered in a fragrant, mildly spiced gravy made from fresh coconut milk, turmeric, green chillies, ginger, garlic, curry leaves, and mustard seeds. Unlike many Indian curries, Meen Moilee is not heavily fried or deeply spiced; the coconut milk is added in stages to create a light, golden-yellow sauce with a creamy texture and a subtle heat. The dish is dairy-free and halal-suitable. Parotta is a South Indian layered flatbread made from refined wheat flour, oil, and salt, kneaded and folded repeatedly to create its characteristic flaky, multi-layered texture. It is cooked on a hot griddle and then beaten to separate the layers. Nutritionally, the combined meal provides moderate protein from the fish (~8 g per 100 g), significant carbohydrate from the parotta (~22.5 g per 100 g), and medium-chain saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.5 g per 100 g). The glycemic index of the composite is approximately 62 (medium), driven primarily by the refined wheat parotta. The sodium content is moderate to high (~310 mg per 100 g), making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, the high glycemic load of the full serving warrants caution; substituting parotta with a lower-GI bread or reducing the parotta portion is advisable. This dish is commonly served at South Indian and Kerala restaurants across Singapore and is popular as a lunch or dinner option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Meen Moilee with Parotta?
Is Meen Moilee good for diabetics?
What fish is used in Meen Moilee?
Is parotta the same as paratha?
How much sodium in Kerala fish curry?
Can I eat Meen Moilee if I have high blood pressure?

ingredient_synonyms.moilee: moily / molly — a Kerala-style coconut milk curry preparation
ingredient_synonyms.parotta: layered flatbread, South Indian parotta (distinct from North Indian paratha)

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Meen Moilee with Parotta as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN reference data for Kerala-style coconut milk fish curry and parotta components at a 55:45 weight ratio. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for refined wheat flatbread and coconut milk curry components. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods data for fish curry (coconut milk base) and parotta, weighted at approximately 55% curry and 45% parotta by plate weight. No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry covers this exact composite dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category; fish may contribute trace amounts but no reliable figure is available. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Meen Moilee with Parotta was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant portioning (200–250 g curry + 2 parotta pieces at ~90–100 g each). 7. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation with fish and plant-based ingredients; individual restaurant practices should be verified. 8. Parotta is distinct from North Indian paratha; it is made from maida (refined wheat flour) and is not gluten-free.
